project_overview.project_name: Nigeria: State Action on Business Enabling Reforms (SABER) Program
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve (1) the efficiency of land administration, (2) the regulatory framework for private investment in fiber optic infrastructure, (3) services provided by investment promotion agencies and public-private partnership units, and (4) the efficiency and transparency of government-to-business services in participating states
project_overview.beneficiaries: All 36 states and Federal Capital Territory (FCT) of Nigeria; private sector firms of all sizes; MSMEs; women entrepreneurs; vulnerable groups

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Nigeria faces significant insecurity challenges, particularly in northern regions. The PAD notes that 'increased insecurity' is a key risk factor affecting Nigeria's growth outlook. However, the SABER program focuses on business-enabling reforms that are applicable across all states regardless of security status. The program is designed to work within existing security constraints.

key_components.component_name: Program-for-Results (PforR) - Result Area 1: Improved Land Administration and Land-Based Investment Process
key_components.description: Supports states to digitize and index Certificates of Occupancy (CofOs), publish land administration procedures, and adopt Framework for Responsible and Inclusive Land-Intensive Agriculture (FRILIA) for sustainable land-based investments. Includes gender-focused initiatives to increase women's land ownership.

key_components.component_name: Program-for-Results (PforR) - Result Area 2: Improved Regulatory Framework for Private Investment in Fiber Optic Infrastructure
key_components.description: Supports states to standardize and reduce Right of Way (ROW) fees to maximum NGN145 per meter and publish transparent ROW processes to facilitate broadband deployment.

key_components.component_name: Program-for-Results (PforR) - Result Area 3: Improved Services Provided by IPAs and PPP Units
key_components.description: Supports development of effective PPP frameworks and strengthening of Investment Promotion Agencies (IPAs) through legal/institutional frameworks, pipeline development, and improved investor services.
key_components.financing: US$167 million (22% of total - DLI 3: US$62m, DLI 4: US$105m)

key_components.component_name: Program-for-Results (PforR) - Result Area 4: Improved Efficiency and Transparency of Government-to-Business Services
key_components.description: Supports transparency of official fees and procedures, simplified tax regimes, grievance redress mechanisms, export promotion, and establishment of small claims courts for commercial dispute resolution.
key_components.financing: US$351 million (48% of total - DLI 5: US$60m, DLI 6: US$89m, DLI 7: US$122m, DLI 8: US$80m)

key_components.component_name: Investment Project Financing (IPF) - Technical Assistance Component
key_components.description: Provides capacity building support to states and strengthens program coordination and verification. Includes hiring of Independent Verification Agent (IVA), Program Management Firm (PMF), and technical consultants.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Ministry of Finance, Budget and National Planning (FMFBNP) through the Home Finance Department (HFD) which houses the Program Coordination Unit (PCU). Presidential Enabling Business Environment Council (PEBEC) provides strategic guidance. States implement reforms through State Steering Councils.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Steering Committee (NSC) chaired by Vice President; PEBEC-NEC Technical Working Group (TWG); State Steering Councils in each participating state; PEBEC-EBES (Enabling Business Environment Secretariat) provides operational support; Nigeria Governors' Forum (NGF) SABER Ad-Hoc Committee
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (4th Edition, November 2020). Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) to be prepared. Advance procurement of key items (IVA, PMF) critical for early implementation. Contracts with high environmental and social risks excluded from Program.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Independent Verification Agent (IVA) verifies achievement of Disbursement-Linked Results (DLRs) annually. Annual Performance Assessment (APA) conducted by IVA. Program Coordination Unit (PCU) responsible for M&E framework and Results Framework monitoring. Mid-term review in second year. Implementation Completion and Results (ICR) report at program closure. Bi-annual Implementation Status and Results (ISRs) reports.

identified_risks.risk_description: General elections in 2023 and state governor elections will likely slow implementation pace. Significant changes in state administrations may lead to reduced political commitment or policy stance changes. Upcoming elections create uncertainty about policy direction.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Continuous engagement with key stakeholders (PEBEC, NGF) before and after elections. Building capacity and strengthening institutions at business-enabling MDAs to minimize post-election disruptions. Business-enabling environment and private sector development likely to remain high priority for any incoming administration given fiscal pressures.

identified_risks.risk_description: Ambitious reforms in the Program remain challenging and require sustained political capital and ownership. Without strong champions, reform implementation may be limited. Stagnation on key federal-level business reforms (macro-financial policies, power sector, trade policy) not covered by Program could reduce overall impact.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Healthy competition among states driven by results-based financing incentivizes achievement. Strong government commitment demonstrated through NEC endorsement. World Bank analytical work on macro-micro dynamics and technical assistance on macroeconomic reforms. SABER platform can become advocacy platform for federal-level reforms.

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Program works across multiple (potentially all 36) states with diverse starting points and capabilities. Inherent complexity and uncertainty from heterogeneous state contexts. Risk of DLI targets not being achieved due to technical or implementation challenges.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Consultative process during preparation. DLI matrix designed to account for state heterogeneity with basic and stretch targets. IPF TA component provides capacity support to states. Peer-learning activities facilitate knowledge transfer. Program restructuring and verification protocol updates recognized as inherent part of implementation.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Federal level has solid competencies (SFTAS experience), but state-level implementation affected by weak capacity of implementing MDAs. High number of key MDAs per state (6 main MDAs plus dozens of others). Multi-sectoral nature requires extensive coordination. State MDAs lack experience with performance-based disbursement environment.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IPF TA component provides key technical assistance and capacity building to business-enabling MDAs. Peer-learning activities enhance knowledge transfer between advanced and lower-capacity states. State Steering Councils (EODB Councils) already established in 17 states. SFTAS experience provides foundation for institutional arrangements.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of delay in transfer of Program funds from federal to state level. Weak internal controls over public expenditures in some states, leading to risk of inflated payroll and ghost workers in less than 25% of states. Weaknesses in compliance with legal/institutional framework for combating fraud and corruption.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Service standards established in Operations Manual to ensure fund transfer within 14 days. SFTAS DLI 5 on payroll management largely addressed ghost worker risk (75%+ states achieved). All states committed through SFTAS Charter to sustain reforms. Annual audited financial statements (IPSAS-compliant) required as Eligibility Criteria. Bi-annual fraud and corruption reporting protocol. Grievance Redress Mechanisms in DLIs 1, 5, 6. World Bank Anti-Corruption Guidelines apply.

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential e-waste generation from digitized CofO databases. Risk of poor FRILIA implementation leading to unsustainable agriculture practices. Indirect displacement from fiber optic network deployment. Potential GBV/SH from influx of workers. Weak E&S systems in most states for managing PPP-related construction/resettlement risks.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: E-waste management strategy to be developed. FRILIA implementation monitored through social audits. Program does not finance construction; private sector fiber deployment limited to existing ROW. E&S considerations mandated in PPP frameworks. Environmental and Social Officers hired at PCU. Capacity building for state staff on E&S management. GRM systems established. Guidelines developed for stakeholder engagement, OHS, GBV/SH prevention.

identified_risks.risk_description: Uncertain economic outlook threatened by Russian-Ukraine war impacts, lower oil production, increased insecurity, election uncertainty, and fiscal risks from PMS subsidy. These factors could reduce private sector investment response to business-enabling reforms.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Program focuses on state-level reforms within government control. World Bank providing analytical support on macro-financial reforms through PASA. Program designed to work within existing macro constraints by focusing on business-enabling environment improvements.

identified_risks.risk_description: Private sector engagement critical but micro/small firms are less organized. Risk of elite capture in land acquisition processes. Potential opposition from vested interests (e.g., those benefiting from haulage fees).
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Organized private sector (NESG, MAN, LCCI) engaged in preparation and represented in NSC/TWG. PEBEC-EBES systematically engages private sector through EODB surveys. DLIs include GRM mechanisms for private sector feedback. Annual business-enabling reforms action planning requires private sector participation. FRILIA implementation includes strong consultative component with community stakeholder engagement.

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: Moderate (for PforR); Low (for IPF TA component)
triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1) - Relevant
Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure (ESS10) - Relevant
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS4) - Relevant
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5) - Not Currently Relevant (but indirectly relevant through FRILIA framework)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6) - Not Currently Relevant
Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (ESS7) - Not Currently Relevant
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Multiple GRM mechanisms established: (1) DLI 1 includes GRM for FRILIA implementation with community grievance redressal and conflict resolution mechanisms; (2) DLI 5 requires operational GRMs at two key business-enabling MDAs with 50-75% of grievances addressed within service level agreements; (3) DLI 6 establishes GRM for traders covering unauthorized payments, harassment, and in-kind requests; (4) Program-level GRM for complaints about Program implementation; (5) World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism available for project-affected communities
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Program addresses gender gap in statutory land rights through: (1) Publication of CofO procedures to increase awareness among women; (2) Collection of gender-disaggregated CofO data; (3) Financial incentives for female-owned or jointly-owned CofO registration; (4) Communications campaigns promoting woman/joint registration; (5) Target to increase female-owned/co-owned CofOs by 15% in Lagos, Edo, and Kano States; (6) Building on Kaduna State's successful pilot (2016-2022) which increased female CofO registrations from 8% to 12%; (7) Gender-sensitive valuations and compensation in land acquisition processes under FRILIA; (8) Protection against disproportionate displacement impacts on women in resettlement
vulnerable_groups: Women (particularly regarding land ownership and tenure security)
Low-income people (affected by fiber optic deployment along ROW)
Micro and small enterprises (MSMEs) - benefit from simplified tax regimes and improved access to justice
Rural communities (affected by land-based investments under FRILIA)
Traders and transporters (affected by trade fee reforms)
Project-affected persons (PAPs) in land acquisition and resettlement contexts

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: The Program is designed to address conflict risks through: (1) Improved land administration and FRILIA framework to reduce land-related conflicts by ensuring transparent, inclusive land-based investment processes; (2) Grievance redress mechanisms to address grievances before they escalate; (3) Community consultation and stakeholder engagement in land acquisition and investment processes; (4) Secure property rights and tenure security to reduce conflicts over land ownership; (5) However, the PAD notes that 'insecurity is also an important aspect affecting Nigeria's business-enabling environment; it falls outside the scope of the areas being supported by the SABER Program.' The Program acknowledges but does not directly address security challenges.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Nigeria is classified as a Fragile State in the World Bank's Systematic Operations Risk-rating Tool (SORT). Key fragility factors mentioned: (1) Increased insecurity with 'recent spike in insecurity in the country' affecting climate vulnerability and economic development; (2) Stagnated development progress since 2015 due to oil price decline, increased insecurity, reversal of macroeconomic reforms, and COVID-19 pandemic; (3) Fiscal pressures and limited fiscal space constraining state governments; (4) Weak institutional capacity at state level; (5) High poverty levels (80 million people living in poverty); (6) Rapid population growth (2.6% per year) widening gap in real GDP per capita; (7) Uncertain economic outlook dependent on external factors and government policy response; (8) Climate vulnerability with exposure to floods, droughts, and desertification
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: The PAD identifies security as a major constraint to private investment but notes it falls outside SABER's scope: 'ongoing security concerns and unfinished reforms of FGN and CBN policies will discourage some types of investment (such as efficiency-seeking FDI).' The Program focuses on state-level business-enabling reforms that can attract domestic investment and market-seeking FDI despite security constraints. Security risks are acknowledged as affecting implementation (e.g., 'increased insecurity' listed as threat to growth outlook) but are not directly addressed by the Program.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Potential indirect displacement: (1) DLI 2 (fiber optic infrastructure) may indirectly lead to 'temporary displacement of people, primarily low-income people selling their wares or residing along the right-of-way for the deployment of fiber optic network.' However, displacement is expected to be limited as deployment will be 'limited to existing ROW' and not financed by the Program (private sector deployment); (2) DLI 3 (PPP framework) may indirectly lead to land acquisition and resettlement for PPP projects implemented after Program closure, with risks of involuntary displacement; (3) DLI 1 (FRILIA) includes land acquisition and resettlement frameworks to manage displacement impacts, with emphasis on compensation, community consultation, and protection of vulnerable groups (especially women). Mitigation measures include: (1) Standard procedures for consultation with program-affected persons along ROW; (2) Effective GRM systems for addressing displacement-related grievances; (3) FRILIA framework ensuring responsible land acquisition with full compensation and resettlement requirements; (4) Gender-sensitive compensation and protection against disproportionate impacts on women
